coredata.dc:title: Highly graphitized 3D network carbon for shape-stabilized composite PCMs with superior thermal energy harvesting
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiao
Gao, Hongyi
Yang, Mu
Dong, Wenjun
Huang, Xiubing
Li, Ang
Dong, Cheng
Wang, Ge
coredata.dc:description: One major barrier obstructing their scale engineered adoption of phase change materials (PCMs), currently, is their low thermal conductivity, which drastically constrains the power capacity. Our target is to enhance the PCMs thermal conductivity without evidently altering other thermal property criteria. Herein, we propose a facile, low-cost and controllable strategy to construct compactly interconnected 3D celosia-like highly graphitized thermally conductive network carbon via carbon quantum dots (CQDs) deriving from acetone and divinyl benzene (DVB). Novel function of CQDs is firstly developed for superior thermal energy harvesting, thus expanding their conventional fluorescence and catalysis field to novel thermal energy storage. Importantly, our constructed 3D graphitized network carbon better infiltrates macromolecule polyethylene glycol (PEG) and fully releases crystallization via controllable crosslinking reaction. This strategy simultaneously integrates sufficient power capacity and incremental thermal conductivity (enhanced by 236%) of the PCMs, and thermal enthalpy is considerably approaching the theoretical value. Alternatively, the composite PCMs are thermally and durably stable. These results indicate that resulting shape-stabilized PCMs are a very promising candidate for renewable thermal energy storage in virtue of the superior comprehensive properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Divinyl benzene
3D network carbon
Phase change materials
Thermal energy storage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of PEG@ 3D porous carbon composite PCMs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of PEG8000@ CQDs-derived 3D porous carbon composite PCMs, (b) TEM images and particle diameter distribution, (c, d) XPS spectrum, (e-g) fluorescent images at different excitation wavelengths of CQDs after cross-linking.
SEM and TEM images of CQDs-derived porous carbon, (a, d) 700 °C, (b, e) 800 °C, (c, f) 900 °C (a–c) before cross-linking, (d–f) after cross-linking, the inset is natural celosia.
Raman spectrum of CQDs-derived porous carbon, (a–c) before cross-linking, (d–f) after cross-linking.
DSC thermogram of PEG8000@ CQDs-derived porous carbon composite PCMs.

Thermal transport evolution of various composite PCMs at the same time during the heating process, (a) 700-0%DVB, (b) 700-3%DVB, (c) 900-3%DVB, (d) time-temperature curves of pure PEG8000 and corresponding PCMs, (e, f) thermal conductivities of PEG8000 @ CQDs-derived porous carbon composite PCMs, (g) melting and freezing enthalpy of PCMs (900-3%DVB) at different cycling times, (h, i) FTIR and XRD spectra of the composite PCMs after 50 times thermal cycling.

head.ce:title: Highly graphitized 3D network carbon for shape-stabilized composite PCMs with superior thermal energy harvesting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Function Materials for Molecule & Structure Construction, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Function Materials for Molecule & Structure Construction, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One major barrier obstructing their scale engineered adoption of phase change materials (PCMs), currently, is their low thermal conductivity, which drastically constrains the power capacity. Our target is to enhance the PCMs thermal conductivity without evidently altering other thermal property criteria. Herein, we propose a facile, low-cost and controllable strategy to construct compactly interconnected 3D celosia-like highly graphitized thermally conductive network carbon via carbon quantum dots (CQDs) deriving from acetone and divinyl benzene (DVB). Novel function of CQDs is firstly developed for superior thermal energy harvesting, thus expanding their conventional fluorescence and catalysis field to novel thermal energy storage. Importantly, our constructed 3D graphitized network carbon better infiltrates macromolecule polyethylene glycol (PEG) and fully releases crystallization via controllable crosslinking reaction. This strategy simultaneously integrates sufficient power capacity and incremental thermal conductivity (enhanced by 236%) of the PCMs, and thermal enthalpy is considerably approaching the theoretical value. Alternatively, the composite PCMs are thermally and durably stable. These results indicate that resulting shape-stabilized PCMs are a very promising candidate for renewable thermal energy storage in virtue of the superior comprehensive properties.

Schematic illustration of PEG8000 @ CQDs-derived 3D porous carbon composite PCMs
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Emerging thermal energy storage of PCMs stimulates a smart, feasible and attention-inspired approach for more energy storage and utilization in virtue of their inherent high volumetric energy density and small temperature variation during latent heat release and retrieval [1–6]. Currently, PCMs are predominantly utilized in thermal energy management system due to their smaller volume evolution and less energy loss [7–10]. Nevertheless, leakage issue and low thermal conductivity of pure PCMs tremendously constrain the energy storage efficiency and charging-discharging ability, thus hindering their broadscale practical utilization. Conventionally, porous supports are employed to prepare shape-stabilized composite PCMs, which guarantee large thermal energy storage capacity, and simultaneously address the leakage of pure PCMs. Preliminary reports have certified that average pore size of the supports is exceedingly essential for thermal properties of shape-stabilized composite PCMs [11,12]. Briefly, if the mean pore size is too small, the PCMs molecular motion might be obstructed; conversely, if it is too large, capillary force is not sufficient to package the liquid PCMs [13]. For instance, Wang and Li have reported that macromolecules PEG are not appropriately infiltrated into some microporous and mesoporous supports with very low enthalpy or even zero due to relatively strong confinement effect on molecule motion [12,14]. Hence, promising supports are anxiously further exploited for infiltrating PEG with large thermal storage capacity and superior charging-discharging ability.

Except for the leakage issue, another major barrier of pure PCMs is their inherent low thermal conductivity, which initiates the hysteresis of thermal response. Adding high-thermal conductive additives is a simple and straightforward method to enhance thermal conductivity, including metal [15], carbon nanotube [16], carbon nanofiber [13] and graphene [17]. However, inadequate additives only obtain limited enhancement of thermal conductivity due to large thermal resistances in the interfacial regions. Ample quantity of additives significantly promotes thermal conductivity with the consequence of obviously undermining energy storage density [18–21]. Moreover, some dispersed additives might be unstable due to interface effect during phase transition [17]. Additionally, surface functionalization of the additive is devoted to reducing the interfacial thermal resistance and promoting the compatibility between the support and the additive. Nevertheless, enhancement effect is still insufficient due to inadequate functionalization [22]. Three-dimensional graphitized network carbon is a promising support candidate for accessing high thermal conductivity, which can provide superior interconnected paths for phonon propagation [23,24]. However, the inherent shortcomings, including tedious preparation, low yield, and high-cost, extremely obstruct their extensive application in thermal energy storage. Hence, it still remains a tremendous challenge to directly prepare such 3D graphitized network carbon.

Herein, novel function of carbon quantum dots is firstly developed for superior thermal energy harvesting, thus expanding their conventional fluorescence and catalysis field to novel thermal energy storage. A facile, low-cost and one-step calcination route was proposed to construct compactly interconnected 3D celosia-like highly graphitized thermally conductive network carbon deriving from CQDs, as shown in Fig. 1a. Controllable pore structure is obtained under the synergistic effect of appropriate pyrolysis temperature and crosslinking reaction. Attractively, our prepared composite PCMs simultaneously integrate high power capacity and incremental thermal conductivity (increased by 236%), ascribing the intensive phonon propagation vibration of sp2-hybrid highly-graphitized interconnected 3D network carbon [25–27]. Moreover, this approach significantly reduces the sacrificing degree of energy storage density, compared with introducing amounts of high-thermal conductivity additives to enhance PCMs thermal conductivity with the consequence of seriously sacrificing storage density. The confinement effect of PEG infiltrated into some microporous and mesoporous supports is also addressed via our constructed 3D mesoporous-macroporous network carbon [12,14]. Additionally, the obtained shape-stabilized composite PCMs are thermally and durably stable, indicating that it is a promising candidate for renewable thermal energy storage in virtue of the excellent comprehensive properties.

Sodium hydroxide (A.R.), acetone (A.R.), HCl (A.R.) and ethanol (A.R.) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Divinyl benzene (Alfa) and polyethylene glycol-8000 (Alfa) were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd.

CQDs was synthesized with some modifications, according to a previous procedure [28,29]. Typically, 5 g sodium hydroxide, 100 mL acetone and different volumes of cross-linker agent DVB (0 mL, 1 mL, 2 mL, 3 mL) are blended to yield aldol reaction under vigorous stirring for 5 h, respectively. Afterwards, all the products were put in the ambient air, room temperature, and atmospheric pressure for 72 h. Then 1 M dilute HCl was used to adjust pH to be neutral. The resulting product was centrifuged and purified with deionized water many times. Finally, the CQDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying at − 70 °C for 24 h.

As described above, all the products after placed for 72 h without any treatment were pyrolyzed for 3 h at diverse temperatures (700, 800, and 900 °C) in N2 atmosphere. Ultimately, the pyrolysis products were purified several times with 1 M HCl solution and deionized water, respectively, and then drying at 100 °C for 12 h to obtain 3D porous carbon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PEG@ 3D porous carbon composite PCMs
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, a physical mixing and impregnation method is utilized to prepare PEG @ 3D porous carbon composite PCMs. First, PEG was completely melted and dissolved in the absolute ethanol for 1 h at 70 °C. Then, porous carbon was introduced into the PEG solution and the resulting mixture was vigorously stirred for 3 h at 70 °C. Finally, the solution product was evaporated at 80 °C for 24 h to yield the required PEG @ porous carbon composite PCMs.

The morphologies of the samples were observed by SEM (ZEISS, SUPRA55) and TEM (JEM-2010). The structure and phase were characterized using XRD (M21X, Cu Ka radiation at 40 kV and 150 mA, λ = 0.15406 nm). The functional groups were evaluated by FTIR (Nicolet 6700) using the KBr pellet technique. The compositions and binding energy were studied through XPS (Thermo escalab 250Xi). Fluorescent images of CQDs were obtained using laser scanning confocal microscope (Olympus FV-1200), at different excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, 546 nm. The pore structure analysis was evaluated through mercury porosimetry (Pore Master GT60). The graphitization degree of porous carbon was evaluated employing Raman (Finder Vista) at the excitation wavelength of 633 nm. Thermogravimetric analysis was performed by TGA (Netzsch STA449F) from 100 to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under N2 atmosphere. Thermal properties of the composite PCMs were characterized by DSC at a heating rate of 10 °C/min and a cooling rate of 10 °C/min under 50 mL/min N2 flow (NETZSCH STA449F3, Germany). Temperature responses of the composite PCMs were recorded at the same time during heating process via an infrared thermal imager HT-02. Thermal conductivity was conducted by a transient plane source technique (Hot Disk TPS 2500S, Sweden) via putting two tablets containing the same kind of PCMs. Temperature-time curves of the samples were recorded using a digital R2100 data collector. The shape-stabilized ability was detected by observing the sample shape evolution at 100 °C with the increase of time, followed by taking the corresponding digital photographs.

To better define the resulting loading value, we perform the leakage measurement. First, the composite PCMs placed on the filter paper were added into an oven for 1 h at 100 °C. At this moment, the phase change of PEG emerged from solid state to liquid state. Afterwards, the composite PCMs were taken out from the filter paper. Ultimately, detailed observation of every piece of filter paper was conducted to determine whether there was any trace of melting PEG, thus further defining the resulting maximum loading content of PEG. The corresponding quantitative change is also measured to obtain more accurate result.

Electron microscopies (SEM and TEM) were utilized to observe the morphology evolution of fabricated carbon materials, as shown in Fig. 2. The distorted lamellar nanosheets can be clearly observed, which is quite similar to the reported graphene. The nanosheets assemble into interconnected networks to generate mesoporous and macroporous celosia-like structure, which is gradually ordered with increasing pyrolysis temperature. Simultaneously, the average thickness of the nanosheets is progressively becoming thinner from 128.3 to 62.1 nm. These results confirm that calcining temperature is crucial for regulating the transformation and assembly during the formation of nanosheets. After cross-linking, more and thinner carbon nanosheets are yielded, interpenetrating porous structure is more homogeneous and distinct, and controllable 3D porous structure is successfully synthesized. When DVB exceeds 3% (vol/vol in acetone), the structure of carbon nanosheets is no longer significantly improved. Hence, DVB content is ultimately determined as 3% in this study. After cross-linking reaction, the average thickness of the nanosheets is raging from 107.2 to 10.0 nm with increasing pyrolysis temperature from 700 to 900 °C. Moreover, the nanosheets still maintain the interconnected network architecture even after high-intensity ultrasound for preparation of TEM samples, indicating the strong connections among the nanosheets. DVB essentially reduces the thickness of the nanosheets and facilitates the self-assembly of the nanosheets into the interconnected network via the crosslinking reaction between DVB and acetone under alkaline conditions. This is because DVB, as cross-linker agent, can regulate multidimensional radical polymerization and bridge the extended carbon chains to construct a cross-linked hyperbranched 3D network polymer [30,31]. During the rapid pyrolysis, the instantaneous high inner pressure is generated, thus effectively restraining the stack of the generated nanosheets and forming thinner nanosheets [32,33]. The thinner nanosheets are more easily and rapidly to self-assembly into the interconnected 3D network through faster curling and intertwining. In principle, constructing the interconnected 3D network carbon with relatively small pores is very valuable for improving thermal storage capacity of PCMs. Additionally, hyperbranched ordered carbon network can provide better thermally conductive paths and consequently enhance the thermal conductivity of the composite PCMs.

TEM images (Fig. 1b) show that typical monodisperse CQDs before calcination are well dispersed. The average diameter of DVB-regulated CQDs is about 2.7 nm. CQDs are still scattered in the carbon nanosheets after calcination (Fig. S3), which may be conducive to the promotion of thermal conductivity of PCMs due to their sp2-hybrid (Fig. 1d) [28]. FTIR (Fig. S2) reveals that as-prepared CQDs contain rich oxygenous functional groups, including C˭O and C-OH. The carbon bonding configurations and elemental composition of as-prepared CQDs were measured by XPS (Fig. 1c), which consist of C (79.97 at%) and O (20.03 at%). In the C1s spectra, the binding peaks of 284.8, 286.7, and 288.9 eV are ascribable to sp2 carbon atoms, alcoholic (C-OH) and carbonyl (C˭O) carbon atoms, respectively. Fluorescent images (Fig. 1e-g) indicate that as-prepared CQDs can emit high-intensity red-green-blue fluorescence due to high electron-hole recombination at different excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, 546 nm, respectively.

Two diffraction peaks of 25° and 44° (Fig. S5) are detected in XRD pattern, which are assigned to diffraction plane indices (002) and (101), indicating a certain degree of graphitization [34]. Raman analysis was performed to further elucidate the graphitization of porous carbon ( Fig. 3). The Raman spectrum shows an sp2-hybrid G -band at 1590 cm-1 and a defect-induced D-band at 1350 cm-1, which associates with the formation of graphitic carbon and the density of phonon states [35–37]. Herein, a progressively narrowing and heightening intensities of G peak with increasing temperature is observed, indicating a gradual transformation from disordered carbon phase to ordered graphitic sp2 hybrid carbon phase [38–40]. Sodium-containing materials, a considerable catalyst, can stimulate carbon graphitization conversion at high temperature pyrolysis [28,41,42]. Raman I
D
/I
G ratios (where I
D and I
G are the Raman integration intensities of D-band and G-band, respectively) are utilized to assess the graphitization quality of carbon material [43–45]. Without DVB, I
D
/I
G ratios are gradually reduced from 3.50 to 2.67, indicating that the graphitization degree is enhanced with elevating temperature. Meanwhile, with DVB, I
D
/I
G ratios also decrease from 3.26 to 1.32, indicating that cross-linking also further promotes graphitization. Hence, highly graphitized 3D porous carbon is prepared under the synergistic effect of appropriate pyrolysis temperature and crosslinking reaction.

The formation mechanism of CQDs and their derivative 3D network porous carbon is schematically proposed in Fig. 1a. The aldol reaction of acetone emerges under the assistance of NaOH, thus yielding unsaturated ketones. Subsequently, the unsaturated ketones undergo polymerization reaction, producing polymer-like products. These stretched carbon chains curl and intertwine, eventually forming a plurality of CQDs [28]. The morphology evolution at diverse calcining stages is monitored to probe the formation mechanism of CQDs-derived 3D porous carbon (Fig. S3). Sodium-containing materials, a considerable catalyst, can stimulate the pyrolysis, weaken the C-O bond and strengthen the reaction potential to further accelerate carbon conversion during fast decomposition process [28,41,42]. Hence, under the collective effect of sodium catalysis and high temperature, the solid solvothermal intermediates are rapidly pyrolysed to yield abundant carbon atoms, which are continuously self-assembled into carbon nanosheets. Ultimately, self-assembly nanosheets present a cross-linked 3D framework structure through curling and intertwining due to the linkage of abundant O-rich functional groups [41]. Introduced DVB, as cross-linker, directs multidimensional radical polymerization. Both vinyl moieties of DVBs are equally reactive and effective radical polymerization results in extended polymerization, thus yielding porous hyperbranched network polymers, which provide better thermal conduction paths and consequently upgrade thermal conductivity of composite PCMs [46–48].

A facile solution impregnation approach is employed to prepare shape-stabilized PCMs. SEM images of these composites with their corresponding largest loading amount (Fig. S9) verify the successful incorporation of PEG8000 inside 3D porous carbon due to the evolution from pristine rough surface to smooth surface, compared with Fig. 2. PEG8000 is well dispersed in the 3D porous structure via surface tension force and capillary action. Additionally, the hydrogen bonding further enhances the interaction between PEG8000 and porous carbon [11]. Hence, the emerging leakage of liquid PEG8000 is effectively inhibited when phase change transition occurs. Nevertheless, it is apparent that some super-large pores were not filled by PEG8000 due to relatively weak capillary force between super-large pores and PEG8000. Typically, relatively large pores often have low absorption capability for melted PCMs due to the weak capillary force between the support and PCMs, thus resulting in low loading content [49,50]. Introduced DVB can effectively reduce the thickness of the nanosheets. The thinner nanosheets are more easily and rapidly to self-assembly into the interconnected smaller pores through faster curling and intertwining. The smaller pores can contribute to larger pore volume, which determines higher loading content of PCMs [51]. Therefore, appropriate DVB regulation is conductive to accessing smaller pores, thus further improving PEG loading. The maximum loading of PEG8000 was determined at 85% in the DVB-regulated 3D porous carbon, in which PEG8000 did not leak over the melting point (Fig. S16 and S17). Meanwhile, XRD patterns (Fig. S7) clarify that there is excellent compatibility between 3D porous carbon and pure PEG8000. This observation indicates that obtained 3D porous carbon is a feasible supporting material for shape-stabilized composite PCMs.

Mercury porosimetry was performed to evaluate the pore structure of the porous carbon and corresponding composite PCMs. Fig. S11a,b illustrates that CQDs-derived porous carbon is mainly composed of mesoporous and macroporous structure, which permits comfortable infiltration of PEG8000 [12,14,50]. More detailed information is exhibited in Fig. S11c. The average pore diameter decreases from original porous carbon of 420 nm to DVB-regulated porous carbon of 250 nm, indicating the effectiveness of cross-linking reaction. Meanwhile, pore volume and specific surface area are greatly improved from 4.78 to 8.40 cm3/g and from 30.48 to 111.62 m2/g, respectively. More importantly, pore analysis of composite PCMs indicates that specific surface area is dramatically reduced and that pore volume is lowered to below 1.5 cm3/g, both revealing that PEG8000 is successfully incorporated into porous carbon. The porous channels with a diameter below 5 µm are infiltrated by PEG8000, while larger pores of more than 5 µm are just partially filled due to the weak capillary force. Additionally, high occupation of relatively small macropore and mesoporore inside porous carbon support is verified by emerging sharp decrease of specific surface area and dramatic increase of mean pore diameter after loading PEG8000. Hence, relatively small macropore and mesoporore is much more appropriate for loading macromolecule PEG8000, thus promoting crystallization behavior release [12,14].

Thermal properties of PEG8000 @porous carbon composite PCMs were measured by means of DSC. The detailed phase change parameters are summarized in Table S1. Phase change enthalpy is the most reliable indicator for assessing the thermal energy storage capacity of the composite PCMs. The fusion and solidification enthalpies (△H m and △H f) of pure PEG8000 are 192.7 and 175.9 J/g, respectively, and the melting and freezing temperatures are 63.89 and 35.60 °C, respectively. Interestingly, the actual △H m and △H f of the composite PCMs is progressively approaching the theoretical △H m and △H f with increasing PEG8000 content. As shown in Fig. 4, more PEG8000 can be loaded inside the porous carbon prepared at elevated temperature whether cross-linking reaction exists or not. Moreover, high loading can establish more homogeneous distribution of PEG8000 inside the 3D porous carbon. Additionally, the composite PCMs illustrates excellent shape-stabilized ability (Fig. S15) and thermal cycling stability (Fig. S14), due to no obvious evolution of melting and freezing enthalpy after different thermal cycling times ( Fig. 5g). Moreover, FTIR and XRD spectra (Fig. 5hi) of the composite PCMs after 50 times thermal cycling are highly consistent with the original FTIR and XRD spectra (Fig. S8 and S7), thus indicating the stability of the composite PCMs during thermal cycling process.

The maximum loading of PEG8000 is 65 wt% before cross-linking, while the maximum is 85 wt% after cross-linking. Interestingly, when the loading is 55 wt%, actual fusion and solidification enthalpies are just 69.4 and 61.4 J/g, respectively, which are extremely smaller than theoretical fusion and solidification enthalpies of 106.0 and 96.7 J/g. Similarly, actual melting and freezing points are 60.36 and 39.44 °C, respectively, which are much smaller than theoretical melting point of 63.89 °C and larger than theoretical freezing point of 35.60 °C. However, at the maximum loading of 85 wt%, actual fusion and solidification enthalpies are 160.3 and 145.8 J/g, respectively, which are exceedingly close to theoretical fusion and solidification enthalpies of 163.8 and 149.5 J/g. Hence, DVB-regulated 3D porous carbon is a promising support for thermal energy storage, both achieving high loading mass and phase change enthalpy.

The impregnation ratio (R) and thermal storage capability (φ), two essential parameters, are employed to evaluate the phase change properties [15]. R and φ can be calculated as follows, where H M, PCM and H F, PCM are the melting and freezing enthalpies of PCMs, respectively; H M, PEG and H F, PEG are the melting and freezing enthalpies of PEG8000, respectively. Actually, impregnation ratio reflects the valid impregnation quantity of PEG8000 inside the 3D porous carbon. The calculated R of PCMs before cross-linking is ranging from 36.0% to 56.8% while R of PCMs after cross-linking is ranging from 63.6% to 83.2%. However, 36.0% and 46.5% are smaller than the theoretical PEG8000 weigh ratio of 55% and 60%, respectively. Interestingly, the outstanding thermal storage capability is acquired in the prepared PCMs after cross-linking because φ is close to 100%, as shown in Fig. S11d,e. The calculated values indicate that PEG8000 molecule chains can be effectively endothermic and exothermic in the DVB-regulated 3D porous carbon during phase change process.

Thermal stability is a very essential evaluation indicator in the development and application of the composite PCMs. The TGA and DTG thermograms of the porous carbon, pure PEG8000 and composite PCMs are illustrated in Fig. S12. It is observed that there is weight loss twice during the heating process. The first slow stage is ascribed to the removal of adsorbed water and the second sharp stage is ascribed to the degradation of PEG molecules, which is very consistent with the similar report [52]. There is no thermal decomposition of PEG molecules when programmed temperature is below 300 °C, which is within the range of PCMs phase change. The starting pyrolysis temperature and finishing temperature of pure PEG8000 is about 330 and 450 °C, respectively. Corresponding to DTG, the fastest weight loss of 65 wt% and 85 wt% PCMs is at 389 and 394 °C, respectively, which is ascribed to the rapid pyrolysis of PEG molecule. The temperature of the fastest losing weight is slightly increasing and the sharp loss rate is progressively increasing with the larger loading content of PEG8000. Integrally speaking, the actual impregnation ratio of PEG8000 relying on the weight loss during the decomposition is basically consistent with the theoretical loading value of PEG8000. 3D porous carbon shows negligible weight loss below 800 °C, implying it is an excellent support of the composite PCMs due to superior thermal stability.

Thermal conductivity of the composite PCMs is also a particularly crucial engineering evaluation factor for commercial feasibility. Herein, transient temperature responses of the composite PCMs were recorded to evaluate their thermal transfer capacity during heating process via an infrared thermal imager, which were taken after a fixed same time on a hot plate at 100 °C. Temperature distribution images (Fig. 5a-c) exhibit that optimized interconnected 3D network carbon after crosslinking reaction facilitates thermal transmission rate. Additionally, the thermal response behavior is more sensitive and temperature increase is much faster at higher calcination temperature, indicating that calcination significantly enhances the thermal conductivity of the composite PCMs. Meanwhile, thermal transfer rates of CQDs-derived and DVB-regulated carbon-based PCMs are both higher than that of pure PEG 8000 (Fig. 5d), which further certify effective enhancement of thermal conductivity. The thermal conduction of non-metallic material is predominantly dominated by thermal vibration of the lattice, namely phonon [53]. Thermal vibration of the phonon in graphitized carbon is intensive, which profits the enhancement of thermal conductivity of composite PCMs. Thermal conductivity of pure PEG8000 is determined to be 0.28 W m-1 K-1. Thermal conductivities of the composite PCMs with corresponding maximum loading amount are evidently presented in Fig. S13. To further substantiate the strengthening effect, the same PEG8000 content is impregnated in the porous carbon (Fig. 5e,f). Thermal conductivities of composite PCMs are progressively raised from 0.69 to 0.91 W m-1 K-1 with increasing calcination temperature, which is highly consistent with the declining I
D
/I
G ratio of the Raman. Compared with pure PEG8000, thermal conductivity of 65 wt% PCMs before cross-linking is increased by 75%, while that of 65 wt% PCMs after cross-linking is enhanced by 236%. Because thermal vibration of the phonon is intensive in sp2-hybridized ordered porous carbon, which favors the promotion of thermal conductivity. The structures of porous carbon are reasonably regulated by calcination and cross-linking reaction, thus promoting the graphitization of 3D networked porous carbon with elevating mean speed and mean free path of the phonon [54–57]. Hence, the thermal conductivity of the composite PCMs is hugely enhanced by 236% due to the intensive thermal vibration of the phonon in the synergistic effect of calcination temperature and crosslinking reaction [25].

Actually, the commercially available conductive paste is not a good conductor. When the conductive paste is very thin, its conductivity at the contact surface is obtained through the "tunneling effect". However, the conductive paste can not be too thick at the contact surface, otherwise it will strongly reduce the conductive effect. Contrastively, our obtained highly graphitized carbon based composite PCMs guarantee excellent thermal transfer and storage performances, thus synergistically preventing the generation of high temperature. However, the main thermal diffusion layer is still pristine thermal diffusion plate. The dominant role of inserted PCM layer in the CPU is to transfer and store thermal energy, thus buffering excessive temperature when the computer CPU temperature rises quickly. These findings can guarantee that ultimate CPU temperature is below 70 °C according to the phase change platform after infiltrating PCMs into the CPU (Fig. S18), thus extending its service life. Similarly, this universal strategy can also be extended to other CPU types via infiltrating diverse phase change temperature PCMs, based on the operating temperature. Hence, our prepared highly graphitized carbon based composite PCMs have great potential in the CPU field.

We propose a facile，low-cost and controllable strategy to construct compactly interconnected 3D celosia-like thermally conductive network carbon via CQDs. Novel function of carbon quantum dots is firstly developed for superior thermal energy harvesting. The effective targeted regulation of the channels of 3D porous carbon is achieved, thus better infiltrating macromolecule PEG and fully releasing crystallization behavior. This strategy simultaneously integrates sufficient power capacity and incremental thermal conductivity of the PCMs, and thermal enthalpy is considerably approaching the theoretical latent heat. Additionally, the obtained shape-stabilized composite PCMs illustrate excellent thermal and durable stability, thus indicating it can be one possibility for renewable thermal energy storage application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51436001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2018.03.075.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiao Chen is currently pursuing his Ph.D. at University of Science and Technology Beijing. His research interests mainly focus on synthesis and application of energy storage and conversion materials.

Hongyi Gao received his Ph.D. in Materials Physics and Chemistry, University of Science and Technology Beijing in 2015. He is now a postdoctoral associate at University of Science and Technology Beijing. His current research focuses on synthesis and characterization of hierarchical oxide nanostructures.

Mu Yang received his Ph.D. in Polymer Physics and Chemistry from Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science (ICCAS) in 2005. Currently he is the professor of material science at University of Science and Technology Beijing. His current research interests focus on porous materials for environmental-friendly applications.

Wenjun Dong received his Ph.D. in Inorganic Chemistry from Jilin University in 2004. Currently he is a professor of School of Materials Science and Engineering at University of Science and Technology Beijing. His recent research interests include synthesis of hierarchical nanostructures with nanoscale precision and exploiting new properties (such as physical, chemical and biological properties) that arise in these new nanoscale architectures.

Xiubing Huang received his Ph.D. in the School of Chemistry at University of St Andrews (UK) in 2015. After one year of postdoctoral work at Research Centre for Materials Science of Nagoya University (Japan), he was appointed as a lecture in the School of Materials Science and Engineering at the University of Science and Technology Beijing. Currently, Dr. Huang has broad interests in nanoporous materials, nanostructured metal oxides, heterogeneous catalysis, energy storage and conversion.
Ang Li is currently pursuing her Ph.D. at University of Science and Technology Beijing. Her research interests mainly focus on synthesis and application of energy storage and conversion materials.

Cheng Dong is currently pursuing his Ph.D. at University of Science and Technology Beijing. His research interests mainly focus on synthesis and application of energy storage and conversion materials.

Ge Wang received her Ph.D. in Chemistry from the Michigan Technological University in 2002. Currently she is a professor and Ph. D. supervisor in the School of Material Science and Engineering at the University of Science and Technology Beijing. In 2012, she became a special chair professor endowed by the Chang Jiang Scholars Program of the Ministry of Education. Her research interests focus on creating complex materials structures with nanoscale precision using chemical approaches, and studying the functionalities including catalytic, energy storage and energy saving properties etc.
